title: Checking ewe condition in late pregnancy
subtitle: How to assess body condition score and adjust feeding in the final six weeks before lambing.
excerpt: A ewe that goes into lambing either too thin or too fat will have problems. Body condition scoring takes two minutes per animal and tells you whether your feeding plan is working. Done at six weeks before the expected lambing date, there is still time to correct.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, BRP manual: Ewe nutrition and body condition scoring. NADIS, ewe nutrition factsheet. SAC Consulting, sheep body condition scoring guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: A numerical score from 1 (emaciated) to 5 (obese) assessing the fat and muscle cover over the spine and ribs of a sheep. Scored by feel rather than sight, since fleece makes visual assessment unreliable. Target for ewes at tupping and late pregnancy is BCS 3 to 3.5.

A metabolic condition in late pregnancy in which a ewe carrying multiple foetuses cannot eat enough to meet her energy requirements, and begins breaking down body fat. The resulting ketone accumulation causes neurological signs and, without intervention, death. Prevented by correct body condition going into late pregnancy and adequate concentrate feeding in the final six weeks.

content.text: A ewe's nutritional needs increase sharply in the final six weeks of pregnancy, when roughly 70 per cent of foetal growth occurs. A thin ewe on flat pasture in January or February will not be able to eat enough to support her foetuses and her own tissue, leading to
. Scoring ewes at six weeks before lambing lets you identify those that need extra concentrate before it is too late to correct.

Restrain the ewe against a wall or between your knees. Place your hand over the spine in the loin region, between the last rib and the hip bone. Press firmly downward and feel the horizontal processes (the ledge-like projections either side of the spine). A

of 3 feels like a firm handshake: the spine is detectable but requires pressure to feel each process. A score of 2 has sharp, prominent processes with little muscle cover below them. A score of 4 feels like a smooth, well-padded surface where the processes are hard to distinguish.
What to do with the score

Ewes at BCS 2 or below at six weeks to lambing need supplementary concentrate feeding in addition to ad lib hay or silage. Ewes at BCS 4 or above should not receive additional concentrate; fat ewes are prone to difficult lambings and metabolic problems in their own right. A ewe carrying triplets needs more energy than one carrying a single, and concentrate allowances should reflect the scan result if you have had ewes ultrasound-scanned.

Watch for ewes separating from the group, standing with their head down, showing star-gazing (neck extended, head pulled back), grinding their teeth, or going off their feed. These are signs of pregnancy toxaemia. Contact a vet promptly; oral propylene glycol is a standard first response and time matters.

Introduce concentrate feeds gradually over 10 to 14 days to allow the rumen microbiome to adjust. A sharp increase in concentrate in the final two weeks can cause acidosis. Split the daily ration into two feeds rather than one to reduce the acidosis risk and improve utilisation.
